policy_statement: Support energy production and methane mitigation from anaerobic digestion using full life-cycle analysis, require methane leakage measurement/abatement, support co-digestion using existing capacity, and require best-practice/low-emissions system designs/retrofits.
mechanism_description: The policy supports expanding anaerobic digestion and includes measurement and abatement of methane leakage so biogas/RNG production is lower-emitting and reduces methane releases while producing energy that can displace fossil generation.
primary_causal_pathway: Waste management practice change → ↓ fugitive CH₄ + biogas/RNG production → fossil energy displacement + ↓ net GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Human activity/system changed: Municipal/industrial organic waste and wastewater solids are diverted/managed through anaerobic digestion systems designed/retrofitted for low leakage, with monitoring, reporting, and mitigation of methane along the biogas chain (digester, gas handling, upgrading, storage).  
(2) Physical process mediating effect: Controlled anaerobic conversion captures methane as usable biogas; leak detection and repair/abatement reduces fugitive methane; combusting biogas/RNG converts CH₄ to CO₂ and provides energy services.  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Net reduction in GHG emissions via avoided methane emissions (high GWP) and displacement of fossil electricity/thermal fuels by biogenic energy (with life-cycle emissions minimized through leakage control).
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reducing net greenhouse-gas emissions by preventing methane releases from organic waste handling and displacing fossil fuel use with captured biogas/RNG.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: The mechanism primarily acts through direct GHG mitigation—capturing/controlling methane and using biogas/RNG to offset fossil energy.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While it can improve waste resource recovery (a resource-efficiency co-benefit), the core climate effect is reduced methane emissions and fossil displacement, which squarely fits Mitigation.  
3) Edge cases applied: If biogas is flared without energy use, mitigation still occurs via methane destruction; if leakage is poorly controlled, benefits can erode, but the mechanism definition explicitly includes measurement/abatement to ensure mitigation dominates.  
4) Applicability to ALL instances: Any policy matching this mechanism (AD expansion + methane leakage measurement/abatement + energy substitution) will have its largest intended climate impact as net GHG reduction.  
5) Secondary subtype deferred: Whether the policy is best categorized more specifically (e.g., waste methane control vs renewable gas fuel switching) depends on policy-level evidence and is deferred.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Potential edge cases include high upstream/downstream methane leakage, use of biogas for non-displacing demand (limited additionality), or feedstock sourcing that induces additional waste generation; these can reduce net benefits but do not change the dominant intended pathway for this mechanism.
key_indicators: “support energy production and methane mitigation”; “full life cycle analysis”; “measurement and abatement of methane leakage”; “Programs that incentivize anaerobic digestion”; “require systems be built (or retrofit) for maximum methane mitigation”; “low emissions biogas or RNG production”
co_benefits: Renewable energy production/energy security; waste diversion and potential odor reduction; possible local air quality improvements (combustion/displacement dependent)
instance_edge_case_notes: Includes regulatory-like conditional requirements (“require…measurement and abatement,” “require…built/retrofit”) but framed as conditions attached to incentivized programs rather than a standalone mandate; could alternatively be split into incentive-program + regulatory-mandate if the broader policy context indicates universal applicability.
